result:
:::writing
The first thing the lighthouse forgot was the sea.

It did not happen all at once. No great wave of absence rolled in to claim it, no sudden hollowing-out like a bell struck too hard. It was smaller than that. Subtler. The kind of forgetting that hides inside routine.

Every night, as it had for nearly a century, the lighthouse woke to its purpose. Its lamp turned with a patient, mechanical grace, casting its eye over the horizon. The keeper—there was always a keeper, though their names changed—would wind the mechanism, polish the glass, and listen to the steady breath of the ocean below.

But one evening, as the light swept its beam across the dark, the lighthouse hesitated.

There was something it was supposed to see.

Something vast, something moving, something alive.

Instead, there was only a long, flat darkness.

The keeper did not notice at first. He was new, and new keepers are full of checklists. Oil levels, wicks, gears, bolts. He moved through the narrow spiral staircase with a mind focused on correctness. If everything was done right, then everything must be right. That is the comfort of systems.

He wound the lamp.

He cleaned the lens.

He recorded the hour.

Outside, the wind moved across the land in a way that was almost, but not quite, like waves.

Days passed. The lighthouse continued its work, though it no longer knew what its work was for. Its beam reached outward into a space that did not answer. Ships did not come. The distant horns that once moaned through fog were gone. Even the gulls, those irreverent witnesses to everything, had disappeared.

The keeper began to notice.

It started with the silence. Not the quiet of night—he knew that well—but a deeper absence. The kind that presses inward. He stood one evening at the balcony, hands gripping the cold iron railing, and tried to remember the sound of the sea.

He could not.

“I must be tired,” he said aloud, as though the lighthouse itself might agree.

The lighthouse did not answer. It had begun forgetting language, too.

Inside its stone walls, memory lingered in fragments. The echo of boots on steps. The faint warmth of hands that had polished its brass. The rhythm of turning light. But the meaning behind these things was slipping, like sand through something that was not quite a hand anymore.

Still, the routine held.

Routine is a powerful scaffold for a fading mind.

Each night, the lamp turned. Each morning, the keeper descended the spiral staircase and marked another day in the logbook.

Day 12: Clear sky. No vessels sighted.

Day 13: Clear sky. No vessels sighted.

Day 14: Clear sky. No vessels sighted.

He paused after writing that one.

“No vessels,” he repeated softly. The words felt strange. As if they described something that had once been possible, but no longer was.

He flipped back through the logbook. The entries grew older, the handwriting shifting from firm to wavering to unfamiliar. Names changed. Ink faded.

Day 8,732: Heavy fog. Two vessels passed eastward.

Day 8,731: Storm conditions. Light maintained.

Day 8,730: Gull nested on the south ledge again.

He touched the page.

“What is a gull?” he asked the empty room.

The lighthouse, if it could have answered, would have said: It is a sound that used to wake us before dawn. It is a shape against the sky. It is proof that there is something beyond us.

But the lighthouse no longer knew what lay beyond its light.

One night, the keeper did something different.

Instead of winding the mechanism, he let it slow.

The great lamp, so faithful in its turning, hesitated. Its beam faltered, then drifted, then stopped entirely, pointing out into the unmoving dark like a finger that had forgotten what it was trying to indicate.

The stillness that followed was immense.

The keeper waited.

He did not know what he was waiting for. That was the strange part. The expectation was there, lodged deep inside him, but its object had been erased.

A sound should have come.

A response.

A consequence.

Nothing did.

Hours passed. The sky remained an unbroken expanse. The horizon did not shift or ripple or gleam. It simply existed, flat and endless and untroubled by anything so dynamic as water.

At last, the keeper sat down on the cold stone floor.

“I think,” he said slowly, carefully, “there used to be something there.”

The lighthouse listened, though it no longer understood listening.

“What was it?” he asked.

And because there was no answer, he tried to build one.

“A forest,” he said. “No. That doesn’t make sense. Forests don’t move like that. I think it moved.”

He closed his eyes, searching the dark behind them for an imprint.

“It moved, and it made a sound. Not like wind. Something deeper. Something… constant.”

The word hovered just out of reach.

Constant.

Like the turning of the light.

Like the rhythm of breath.

Like something that had been there long before the lighthouse, and would have remained long after.

But it had not remained.

Or perhaps it had, and the lighthouse had simply forgotten how to see it.

The keeper stood and walked to the edge once more. Without the light, the darkness felt thicker, more real. He leaned forward slightly, as though proximity might reveal what distance had hidden.

“I will remember,” he said, though he had no idea how.

Behind him, the lamp sat still. Its gears, unwound, rested in a quiet that was not quite peace.

Time, which had always been measured in rotations, became uncertain.

Minutes passed, or hours, or something else entirely.

And then—

A sound.

Faint. Almost imperceptible.

The keeper froze.

There it was again.

Not wind.

Not silence.

Something between.

He held his breath, as if that might help him hear.

The sound grew, not louder, but clearer. A low, steady murmur. A repetition without exact repetition. A motion contained within sound itself.

His heart responded before his mind did.

“Yes,” he whispered. “Yes, that’s it.”

The lighthouse, deep in its stone and iron and memory, stirred.

The sound filled in the missing space. It shaped the darkness into something with depth, with movement, with life.

The keeper stepped back inside and seized the winding handle.

The mechanism resisted at first, stiff from disuse, but then it yielded. The gears caught. The lamp shuddered, then began its slow, deliberate turn.

Light spilled outward once more.

This time, when it reached the horizon, it found something waiting.

Not clearly. Not fully. But enough.

A shifting surface.

A vast, breathing expanse.

The sea.

The lighthouse did not remember it all at once. It remembered in pieces. A reflection. A motion. A purpose.

The beam swept again, and the sea answered, not with words, but with presence.

The keeper laughed—a small, startled sound, as though he had tripped over something precious he didn’t realize he’d dropped.

“There you are,” he said.

And though the lighthouse could not yet speak, if it could have, it might have replied:

There you are, too.
:::writing
